title: Space Gets Crowded!
story: Zoom! On December 2, 1990, space became a bustling place. For the first time ever, twelve astronauts floated in orbit together. Two space shuttles, a Soviet spacecraft, and a space station hosted this cosmic crowd. Imagine the excitement as these explorers waved to each other across the vast darkness, sharing a unique moment in human history. It was like a stellar party, with Earth as the glittering backdrop!
activity_question: How many astronauts were in space at the same time?
follow_up_question: What types of spacecraft were involved in this space record?

title: China's Lunar Leap: Jade Rabbit Hops to Moon
story: Blast off! China's space program took a giant leap on December 2, 2013, when it launched its first-ever moon rover mission. The spacecraft, named Chang'e-3, carried a robotic explorer called Yutu, or 'Jade Rabbit' in English. This plucky little rover was designed to scamper across the lunar surface, snap photos, and dig into moon dirt. It marked a major milestone for China's space ambitions, joining an elite club of nations to land on Earth's celestial neighbor.
activity_question: What was the name of China's first moon rover?
bonus_fact: Yutu means 'Jade Rabbit' in English, inspired by a mythical rabbit in Chinese folklore.

title: Sprinting Through 18 Holes
story: Imagine golfing at lightning speed! On December 2, 1982, American track star Steve Scott raced through a round of golf like never before. At Miller Golf Club in California, he zipped through 18 holes in just under half an hour. Scott used only two clubs and sprinted between shots, finishing with a score of 95. His speedy play set a new record for the fastest regulation round of golf ever.
activity_question: What did Steve Scott use during his record-breaking golf round?
follow_up_question: Why do you think Steve Scott ran between holes instead of walking or using a golf cart?

title: Hawking's Warning: AI's Potential Danger
story: Renowned scientist Stephen Hawking made waves with a startling statement. In 2014, on December 2, he expressed concern about the future of artificial intelligence. Hawking suggested that AI might pose a serious risk to humanity. He believed advanced AI could potentially outpace and outsmart humans, leading to unforeseen consequences. This warning from such a brilliant mind sparked global discussions about the responsible development of AI technology.
activity_question: What did Stephen Hawking suggest about artificial intelligence?
activity_choices: It could pose a serious risk to humanity
It would solve all human problems
activity_answer: It could pose a serious risk to humanity
follow_up_question: Why do you think Hawking was concerned about AI?

title: The Birth of Nuclear Power
story: Beneath a squash court in Chicago, scientists made history. On December 2, 1942, a team led by Enrico Fermi and Leó Szilárd achieved the world's first self-sustaining nuclear chain reaction. This groundbreaking experiment took place in Chicago Pile-1, the planet's first nuclear reactor. It marked the dawn of the atomic age, opening doors to new energy sources and scientific discoveries.
activity_question: Where did the first self-sustaining nuclear chain reaction occur?
activity_choices: A secret lab in the desert
follow_up_question: What do you think scientists hoped to achieve with this experiment?

title: A Heart of Steel Beats On
story: In 1982, a groundbreaking medical miracle occurred. Barney Clark, a 61-year-old retired dentist from Seattle, received the world's first permanent artificial heart. On December 2, Dr. William DeVries led the pioneering surgery at the University of Utah Hospital. The Jarvik-7 heart, a marvel of medical engineering, kept Clark alive for over three months, paving the way for future advancements in cardiac care.
activity_question: What was the name of the artificial heart implanted in Barney Clark?
follow_up_question: Why do you think artificial hearts are important for medical science?

title: Earth Swallows a Giant
story: The ground suddenly gave way in Alabama, creating a massive hole that swallowed trees and earth. This wasn't just any sinkhole - it was the 'December Giant', the largest sinkhole ever recorded in the United States. On December 2, 1972, the earth opened up, leaving a gaping chasm. Sinkholes form when underground water erodes rock, causing the surface to collapse. This event reminded everyone of the hidden forces shaping our planet.
activity_question: What was the name given to this record-breaking sinkhole?
follow_up_question: Why do sinkholes form?

title: Ancient Skull Unearths Human History
story: Buried deep in the caves of Zhoukoudian, a groundbreaking discovery awaited. In 1929, scientists unearthed the first skull of Peking man, a fascinating ancestor of modern humans. This remarkable find happened on December 2, in caves about 50 km from Peking, China. The skull turned out to be incredibly old, dating back roughly 750,000 years!
activity_question: Where was the first Peking man skull discovered?
activity_choices: Under the Great Wall of China
At the bottom of the Yellow River
follow_up_question: Why do you think scientists were excited about finding such an old human skull?

title: Mars 3: A Space Pioneer's Gentle Landing
story: Zoom! A tiny spacecraft named Mars 3 rocketed through space, aiming for the Red Planet. On December 2, 1971, it made history. This Soviet probe became the first to softly touch down on Mars. title: A Lifeline for Children in Danger
story: Fleeing danger, a train full of hope chugged into Britain in 1938. This was no ordinary journey. It carried precious cargo: Jewish children escaping Nazi Germany. Known as the Kindertransport, it was the first of many rescue missions that would save thousands of young lives. As the children stepped onto British soil on December 2, they found safety and a chance for a brighter future.

title: Ancient Bones Reveal Human Ancestor
story: Bones can tell amazing stories! In 1927, scientist Davidson Black made a thrilling discovery. He found ancient human-like fossils in Zhoukoudian, China. On December 2, he announced these bones belonged to a new species. He called it 'Sinanthropus Pekinensis', or Peking Man. Today, we know this ancestor as Homo Erectus. It's like finding a missing piece in the puzzle of human history!
activity_question: What did Davidson Black call the new species he discovered?
follow_up_question: Why do you think scientists give special names to fossil discoveries?

title: Ancient Human Ancestor Found in Africa
story: Buried for over a million years, a remarkable discovery was unearthed in 1960. On December 2, paleoanthropologist Louis Leakey found an ancient human ancestor in Tanzania's Olduvai Gorge. This fossil, named Olduvai Hominid 9, belonged to Homo erectus and was about 1.4 million years old. It provided crucial clues about human evolution and our distant past.
activity_question: What type of ancient human ancestor did Louis Leakey discover?
follow_up_question: Why do you think scientists study fossils like the one Louis Leakey found?

title: A Giant Eye for the Stars
story: Imagine a mirror so big it could see farther into space than ever before! On December 2, 1934, scientists poured molten glass to create a massive telescope mirror. At over 5 meters wide, this mirror would become the eye of the Mount Palomar Observatory. It took years to cool and polish, but when finished, it opened up new views of distant galaxies and helped astronomers uncover secrets of the universe.
activity_question: What was special about the mirror cast on December 2, 1934?
activity_choices: It was shaped like a star

title: Powering Up: America's Nuclear Milestone
story: Atoms lit up homes in 1957! The first big nuclear power plant for everyday use in America fired up in Shippingport, Pennsylvania on December 2. Scientists and engineers worked hard to harness the atom's energy safely for peaceful purposes.

title: Birth of a Desert Nation
story: Six tiny kingdoms joined forces to create a powerful new country. Abu Dhabi, Ajman, Dubai, Fujairah, Sharjah, and Umm Al Quwain united as the United Arab Emirates in 1971. On December 2, they declared independence from the United Kingdom, transforming from the Trucial States into a thriving nation. Today, the UAE is known for its towering skyscrapers, luxurious shopping, and rich cultural heritage.
activity_question: Which six kingdoms united to form the United Arab Emirates?
activity_choices: Abu Dhabi, Ajman, Dubai, Fujairah, Sharjah, and Umm Al Quwain
Cairo, Alexandria, Luxor, Aswan, Giza, and Suez
Riyadh, Jeddah, Mecca, Medina, Dammam, and Tabuk
Muscat, Salalah, Sohar, Sur, Nizwa, and Duqm
activity_answer: Abu Dhabi, Ajman, Dubai, Fujairah, Sharjah, and Umm Al Quwain
bonus_fact: The UAE's flag colors represent Arab unity, peace, hope, and courage.

title: Napoleon's Brilliant Victory at Austerlitz
story: Cannons roared and swords clashed as Napoleon Bonaparte led his French army to a stunning triumph. On December 2, 1805, he outsmarted the combined forces of Russia and Austria at the Battle of Austerlitz. Napoleon's clever tactics fooled his enemies, luring them into a trap. This victory cemented Napoleon's reputation as a military genius and changed the balance of power in Europe.
activity_question: What did Napoleon's victory at Austerlitz do for his reputation?
activity_choices: Cemented his reputation as a military genius

Showed he was good at painting

title: Joey Votto: Canada's Baseball Hero
story: Batter up! On December 2, 2016, Joey Votto knocked it out of the park again. The Cincinnati Reds first baseman won the Tip O'Neill Award as the best Canadian player in Major League Baseball. It was his sixth time claiming the title in seven years! Votto's stellar season included a .326 batting average and a league-leading on-base percentage of .434.
activity_question: What award did Joey Votto win in 2016?
bonus_fact: The award is named after Tip O'Neill, one of the first Canadians to play in the major leagues.

title: Cricket Captain's Spectacular Debut
story: Greg Chappell made a splash as Australia's new cricket captain in 1975. In his first match leading the team, he scored an amazing 123 runs in the first innings. But he wasn't done yet! Chappell then hit 109 more runs in the second innings, helping Australia win by 8 wickets against the West Indies in Brisbane on December 2.
activity_question: What team did Australia defeat in Greg Chappell's debut as captain?
follow_up_question: How many runs did Greg Chappell score in total across both innings?

title: Boxing Champ's Knockout Victory
story: Fists flew in London as Canadian Tommy Burns defended his world heavyweight boxing title. On December 2, 1907, Burns faced off against England's Gunner Moir at the National Sporting Club. The crowd watched in awe as the two fighters battled for ten intense rounds. In the end, Burns' powerful punches proved too much for Moir, securing a knockout victory and keeping his championship belt.
activity_question: Where did the boxing match take place?
follow_up_question: What country was Tommy Burns from?

title: Cricket's Controversial 'Bodyline' Battle Begins
story: Bats clashed with balls as the infamous 'Bodyline' cricket series kicked off in Sydney in 1932. Australia faced England's fierce bowling tactics on December 2, ending the day at 290 runs for 6 wickets. Stan McCabe stood strong, scoring 127 not out. England's Harold Larwood took 4 wickets with his notorious fast bowling. The match sparked debate about fair play in sports.
activity_question: Who scored 127 not out for Australia on the first day?
follow_up_question: Why do you think the 'Bodyline' series was considered controversial?

title: Dutch Rocket Soars to New Heights
story: Zoom! A rocket blasted off in the Netherlands, reaching an impressive height of 10 kilometers. This wasn't just any launch - it was the very first Dutch rocket to take to the skies. On December 2, 1963, Dutch scientists and engineers celebrated as their hard work paid off in a spectacular display of technology and innovation.
activity_question: How high did the first Dutch rocket reach?
follow_up_question: Why do you think it's important for countries to develop their own rocket technology?
